provenance.source_corpus: Long‐term effects of prairie restoration on plant community structure and native population dynamics

source_spans.text: plots that received supplemental seeding continued to exhibit higher richness of native species than those left unseeded
plots that received supplemental seeding continued to exhibit higher richness of native species than those left unseeded
addition of native seed effectively increased both the richness and abundance (defined as percent cover) of native species
both seeding and disturbance treatments could positively influence native species abundance over the long term
treatments that included grass-specific herbicide were the most effective at reducing the abundance of exotic grasses
three perennial forb species (Eriophyllum lanatum, Achillea millefolium, and Ranunculus occidentalis) responded more positively to both of the burn treatments compared with other treatments
treatments that included grass-specific herbicide were the most effective at reducing the abundance of exotic grasses
three perennial forb species (Eriophyllum lanatum, Achillea millefolium, and Ranunculus occidentalis) responded more positively to both of the burn treatments compared with other treatments
three perennial forb species (Eriophyllum lanatum, Achillea millefolium, and Ranunculus occidentalis) responded more positively to both of the burn treatments compared with other treatments
three perennial forb species (Eriophyllum lanatum, Achillea millefolium, and Ranunculus occidentalis) responded more positively to both of the burn treatments compared with other treatments
native grass species, Festuca roemeri, had greater cover in treatments that included the grass-specific herbicide sethoxydim
there is evidence that all eight species colonized new plots since the last round of monitoring
